content:
# Genesis Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11v–12r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_021_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_021_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0020

---

## The Child of Laughter

**1** The LORD kept his word to Sarah. He did exactly what he had promised. **2** Sarah became pregnant and gave birth to Abraham's son — in his old age, at the exact time God had told him it would happen. **3** Abraham named the boy Isaac. **4** When Isaac was eight days old, Abraham circumcised him, just as God had commanded.

**5** Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born.

**6** Sarah said, "God has given me something to laugh about — everyone who hears about this will laugh right along with me." **7** She said, "Who would ever have told Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet here I am — I have given him a son in his old age."

---

**8** The child grew and was weaned. On the day of Isaac's weaning, Abraham threw a great celebration. **9** But Sarah saw Hagar's son — the boy born to Abraham from the Egyptian woman — laughing. **10** She said to Abraham, "Throw out this slave woman and her son. The son of this slave is not going to share the inheritance with my son Isaac. Not a chance."

**11** This deeply distressed Abraham, because it concerned his own son. **12** But God said to Abraham, "Don't be troubled about the boy or about Hagar. Do whatever Sarah tells you, because it is through Isaac that your family line will carry your name. **13** But I will also make a great nation out of the slave woman's son — because he too is your child."

**14** Abraham got up early the next morning. He took bread and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar, placing them on her shoulders, along with the boy. Then he sent her away. She walked off and wandered in the desert of Beer-sheba.

**15** When the water in the skin was gone, she pushed the boy under one of the bushes. **16** Then she walked away and sat down some distance off — about a bowshot away — because she said to herself, "I cannot watch the boy die." She sat down at a distance and began to sob.

**17** God heard the boy crying. The angel of God called out to Hagar from the sky: "What is the matter, Hagar? Don't be afraid. God has heard the boy crying right where he is. **18** Get up, pick up the boy, and hold him close. I am going to make him a great nation."

**19** Then God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water. She went and filled the skin and gave the boy a drink.

**20** God was with the boy as he grew up. He settled in the desert and became a skilled archer. **21** He lived in the Desert of Paran, and his mother found him a wife from Egypt.

---

**22** At that time, Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his forces came to Abraham and said, "God is with you in everything you do. **23** Now swear to me here before God that you will not deal falsely with me, or with my children, or with my descendants. Show me the same loyalty I have shown you in this land where you have lived as a foreigner."

**24** Abraham said, "I swear it."

**25** Then Abraham confronted Abimelech about a well that Abimelech's servants had seized. **26** But Abimelech said, "I have no idea who did this. You never told me about it, and I heard nothing until today."

**27** Abraham brought sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a covenant. **28** Abraham also set apart seven ewe lambs from the flock, **29** and Abimelech asked him, "What are these seven ewe lambs you have set apart by themselves?"

**30** Abraham answered, "Accept these seven lambs from my hand. They will serve as proof that I dug this well."

**31** So that place was called Beer-sheba — because it was there that the two of them swore an oath together.

**32** After they had made the covenant at Beer-sheba, Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his forces returned to the land of the Philistines. **33** Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beer-sheba, and there he called out to the LORD, the Everlasting God. **34** And Abraham lived as a foreigner in the land of the Philistines for a long time.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 21 — the chapter laughter built

The name *Isaac* — יִצְחָק (*yitschaq*) — means "he laughs." The entire chapter is soaked in that name and everything it carries. It begins with the laughter of joyful impossibility (v.6), carries through the ambiguous laughter of a scene that nearly tears the family in two (v.9), and settles finally into a story of two nations going their separate ways, each with a future. The wordplay is not decorative. It is the spine of the chapter.

---

### Verses 1–2 — the kept promise

**וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת-שָׂרָה כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמָר** — "The LORD visited Sarah as he had said."

The verb **פָּקַד** (*paqad*) is one of the richest words in the Hebrew Bible. It means to visit, attend to, appoint, take note of, remember in an active sense. It carries within it the idea that God has not been absent — God has been *watching*, and now the moment of action has arrived. The NRSV renders it "The LORD dealt with Sarah"; the KJV has "The LORD visited." I have translated "The LORD kept his word" because what the verse most wants to say, in context, is not simply that God showed up but that the promise made so specifically back in chapter 18 — *at the appointed time, this time next year* — has now arrived. The visit *is* the fulfillment. The word kept is the word visited.

The double confirmation in verse 1 — "he did exactly what he had promised" — is the text's insistence: this is not coincidence. This is the covenant arriving on schedule.

---

### Verse 3 — naming Isaac

**וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ הַנּוֹלַד-לוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה-לּוֹ שָׂרָה יִצְחָק** — "Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him, Isaac."

The sentence piles up relative clauses to say: this son — the one born to him, the one Sarah bore him — this is the one named Isaac. The name is stated at the end of a sentence that insists, with accumulated care, on his identity. This boy is the son of promise. Every word of identification makes that more sure.

---

### Verse 6 — Sarah's laughter transformed

At Genesis 18:12, Sarah laughed inside herself when she overheard the promise of a son: **וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה בְּקִרְבָּהּ** — "Sarah laughed to herself." It was the laugh of incredulity, of a woman too old and too realistic to believe what she was hearing. God heard it and challenged her, and Sarah denied laughing. She was afraid.

Now the same root *tsechoq* returns in verse 6, but everything has changed. **צְחֹק עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים** — "God has made laughter for me." The laugh is no longer a private, hidden, ashamed reaction. It has become a gift that overflows — *everyone who hears will laugh with me*. Sarah's laughter has been redeemed. God did not take away the laugh; God transformed it. What was once the laugh of disbelief is now the laugh of impossible joy.

The wordplay is sustained through verse 7: "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children?" — **מִי-מִלֵּל לְאַבְרָהָם** — the incredulity is still there, but it has been turned inside out. The same thing that was *unbelievable* is now *wonderful* because it has actually happened. This is what fulfilled promises feel like: you knew they were unlikely, and now the unlikeliness makes them more glorious, not less.

---

### Verse 9 — the word that changes everything: metsacheq

**וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת-בֶּן-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם מְצַחֵק** — "Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, who had borne him to Abraham, *metsacheq*."

The word **מְצַחֵק** (*metsacheq*) is the Piel participle of the same root as Isaac's name — *tsechoq*, laughter. Its basic meaning is "laughing, playing, joking." But the Piel stem can intensify or color the action, and the word appears in contexts ranging from innocent play (children playing) to sexual suggestiveness (Gen 26:8, where Isaac himself "caresses" Rebekah) to outright mockery (Judg 16:25, where Samson is brought out to "perform" for the Philistines).

What exactly was Ishmael doing?

The Codex Vaticanus LXX (which I can read directly in the manuscript image before me) adds the phrase **μετὰ Ἰσαὰκ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς** — "with Isaac her son" — making it "playing with her son Isaac." This addition appears to have been a clarifying gloss in one strand of the LXX tradition, specifying the playmate. The Leningrad Codex Hebrew does not include it. The Peshitta and Targums also add clarifications in different directions.

The ambiguity in the Hebrew is almost certainly intentional. The narrator gives us only the bare fact: Ishmael was *metsacheq* — doing the thing the name Isaac is built on. Was he playing — joyfully, innocently? Was he mocking — taunting the infant, perhaps mimicking his name in derision? Was there a sexual overtone, as rabbinic interpreters later suggested, reading this in light of Genesis 26:8?

The text does not say. It lets Sarah's reaction carry the weight. She saw *something* in Ishmael's laughing or playing — something that alarmed her enough to demand the boy and his mother be driven out. That reaction may be maternal protectiveness, or it may be jealousy, or it may be a genuine perception of threat. The text will not resolve it for us.

I have translated simply "laughing" — which holds the ambiguity open. The reader stands where Sarah stood: watching a scene that could be read multiple ways, and trying to understand why it troubled her so deeply.

---

### Verse 10 — Sarah's demand and the covenantal stakes

**גָּרֵשׁ הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת וְאֶת-בְּנָהּ** — "Drive out this slave woman and her son."

Sarah's words are hard. They use the verb **גָּרַשׁ** (*garash*), which means to expel, to drive out forcibly — the same verb used later for expelling nations from the land. There is no softness in it. And her reasoning is equally blunt: "the son of this slave is not going to share the inheritance with my son."

The word for "slave" or "slave woman" throughout this passage is **אָמָה** (*amah*) — a term that emphasizes Hagar's dependent, unfree status. This is not affectionate. It is a categorization. Sarah is drawing a social and legal line: that woman is a slave; her son is the son of a slave; he has no standing equal to my son's.

What makes this theologically complicated is that God agrees with her. Verse 12 — "Do whatever Sarah tells you" — is not an endorsement of Sarah's tone or of the institution of slavery. But it is a divine confirmation that the covenant inheritance flows through Isaac. Paul will later read this episode as an allegory (Galatians 4:30), quoting Sarah's words almost verbatim as the voice of scripture itself. The harshness of the expulsion serves the theological architecture: two families, two futures, one covenant line.

---

### Verses 11–13 — Abraham's grief and God's reassurance

**וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם** — "The thing was very evil in the eyes of Abraham."

The word **רַע** (*ra'*) means evil, bad, harmful, wrong. Abraham is not mildly inconvenienced. He is distressed in a way the text characterizes with moral weight: what Sarah is asking him to do registers, to him, as *wrong*. He has loved this boy. Ishmael is his son too. The Greek Vaticanus renders this ἐσκληρύνθη δέ — "it was a hard thing" — which softens the moral force somewhat. The Hebrew is sharper: Abraham thought it was *wrong*.

God's reassurance works on two levels. First, it is a theological statement about covenant: Isaac carries the line. Second, it is a promise about Ishmael: **גַּם אֶת-בֶּן-הָאָמָה לְגוֹי אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ** — "I will also make a nation of the slave woman's son." The *gam* — "also," "even" — is significant. God is not dismissing Ishmael. God is redirecting his future. Ishmael has a future too, but it is not the covenant future of Isaac. Two sons, two futures, both under God's watch.

---

### Verses 14–16 — Hagar's desperate journey

The expulsion scene is told with economy and piercing detail. Abraham gets up early. He takes bread and a skin of water. He places them on Hagar's shoulder. The boy goes with them. He sends them off.

One detail in the Hebrew of verse 14 is contested: **וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הָגָר שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ** — literally "he gave to Hagar, placing on her shoulder." Some ancient interpreters read the Hebrew as implying Abraham placed the boy *on her shoulder* as well — which would strain against the detail that Ishmael must have been a teenager at this point (born before Isaac, who is being weaned at perhaps age 2-3, and Sarah's reference in 16:16 that Abraham was 86 when Ishmael was born). The LXX Vaticanus text appears to try to resolve this by rendering it in a way that has the bread and water on her shoulder and the child led by hand. The Hebrew is slightly ambiguous; I have followed the most natural reading.

What undoes me in this passage is verse 15: **וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם** — "she thrust the boy under one of the bushes." The verb **שָׁלַךְ** (*shalach*) means to throw, cast, hurl. This is not a gentle laying down. The water is gone. The boy is dying. Hagar cannot carry him any further and cannot watch him die, so she puts him under a bush — the only shade she can give him — and walks away. She sits at a bowshot's distance and weeps.

The image of a mother who cannot watch her child die is one of the most powerful in all of Genesis. No commentary is needed.

---

### Verse 17 — "God heard the boy" — Ishmael's name fulfilled

**וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר** — "And God heard the voice of the boy."

The name **יִשְׁמָעֵאל** (*Yishmael*) means "God hears." It was given to him before his birth (Genesis 16:11) precisely because "the LORD has heard your affliction." Now, at the lowest point of his life — dying of thirst in the desert, cast out by his father's household — the name comes true again. God hears. The name is the promise; the promise is fulfilled.

The angel speaks to Hagar: "What is the matter, Hagar?" — **מַה-לָּךְ הָגָר** — the same opening phrase the angel used to her at the spring in the desert in chapter 16, when she had fled Sarai's harsh treatment. The same woman, twice in the wilderness, twice encountered by heaven.

The instruction is stunning in its simplicity: "Get up. Pick up the boy. Hold him close." And then the great reversal: **כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ** — "because I am going to make him a great nation." The child who was under a bush to die is the ancestor of a great nation. God opens Hagar's eyes — the same verb used when scales fall from blind eyes — and she sees a well. She fills the skin. The boy drinks.

---

### Verse 20 — Ishmael's life

**וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַנַּעַר** — "God was with the boy."

This brief statement, tucked quietly between the rescue and the geography, is a theological summary of Ishmael's life. He grows up. God is with him. He becomes an archer in the wilderness. His mother finds him a wife from Egypt — her own land, the land she came from. The man born of an Egyptian slave woman returns, through his marriage, to Egyptian stock.

Ishmael's story in Genesis does not end in rejection. It ends with God's presence, with a future, with a family. He and Isaac will bury their father together in chapter 25. The text's treatment of Ishmael is more generous than many readings of it have been.

---

### Verses 22–24 — Abimelech's treaty request

The second half of the chapter shifts gear entirely. The desperation of the wilderness gives way to the formality of diplomacy. Abimelech and his military commander Phicol come to Abraham with a proposal: swear that you will deal with me and my people fairly, because it is obvious that God is with you.

**כִּי-אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה** — "God is with you in everything you do."

The pagan king sees what the narrative has shown us across all of Abraham's story: divine accompaniment. Whether Abraham is acting with extraordinary faith or stumbling badly, the presence of God is visible in his life from the outside. This is a remarkable testimony coming from a Philistine ruler.

The request for a treaty is practical as well as theological. Abimelech wants to lock in the relationship while he has leverage — while Abraham is still in his territory, still dependent on Philistine hospitality for access to wells and grazing land. Abraham agrees immediately.

---

### Verses 25–31 — Beer-sheba: the well of the oath

The etymology of **בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע** (*Beer-sheba*) is a double wordplay the text builds carefully. The name holds two meanings simultaneously:

1. **בְּאֵר** (*be'er*) = well + **שֶׁבַע** (*sheva'*) = seven: the "well of seven," named for the seven ewe lambs Abraham sets apart as a formal witness to his ownership
2. **בְּאֵר** (*be'er*) = well + **שָׁבַע** (*shava'*) = to swear an oath: the "well of the oath," named for the covenant sworn there

Both meanings are active in verse 31: **עַל כֵּן קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בְּאֵר שָׁבַע** — "therefore that place was called Beer-sheba." The "therefore" connects backward to *both* — to the seven lambs *and* to the oath. The well is the place where something was proven (seven witnesses, seven lambs) and where a solemn promise was made. The town's name carries that double foundation.

I have preserved "Beer-sheba" as a place name throughout rather than translating it, since it is still a city in the Negev and since the etymology is itself part of what the text is doing. A single English translation would collapse the wordplay.

---

### Verse 33 — YHWH El Olam: the Everlasting God

**וַיִּקְרָא-שָׁם בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה אֵל עוֹלָם** — "And he called there on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God."

**עוֹלָם** (*olam*) means what endures, what has always been and always will be, the age that stretches without end in both directions. The phrase **אֵל עוֹלָם** — "God of Eternity," "God of the Ages," "the Everlasting God" — appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible, though the concept permeates the Psalms and prophetic literature. It is significant that Abraham calls God by this name here, at Beer-sheba, at the end of a chapter that has been about futures — Isaac's future, Ishmael's future, the futures of two nations, the futures of wells and trees and cities. To plant a tree and call on the Eternal God is to say: what is happening here is not temporary. These covenants, these children, these wells — they belong to the God who outlasts all of them.

The tamarisk tree (*'eshel*, אֵשֶׁל) is a practical choice for the Negev: it is drought-resistant, long-lived, and grows deep roots in dry ground. Abraham plants what will outlast him, at a place named for an oath, and calls on the God who outlasts everything. The chapter ends not with drama but with roots.

---

### The chapter as a whole: two sons, two promises

Genesis 21 is a chapter about division and provision. The covenant family divides — Isaac is kept, Ishmael is sent away — but both sons receive divine provision. Isaac receives it through the covenant line; Ishmael receives it through God's direct hearing of a dying boy's cry in the desert. God is not absent from the expelled story. God is present in both branches.

This is a recurring pattern in Genesis: the chosen line and the unchosen line both receive divine attention, but in different modes. Abel and Cain. Shem and Ham. Isaac and Ishmael. Jacob and Esau. The text does not pretend the choices are without cost or pain. Sarah's hardness, Abraham's distress, Hagar's grief — none of this is sanitized. But the pattern insists: the God who elects does not abandon. The ones sent away are not abandoned by heaven, even when they are separated from the covenant community.

The wordplay on *tsechoq* — laughter — ties everything together. Sarah laughed in disbelief; God gave her laughter in joy. Ishmael was *metsacheq* — doing something with that same root word — and the doing of it set in motion the expulsion. The boy of laughter (*yitschaq*) remains; the boy who laughed (*metsacheq*) departs. But even in departing, he is heard (*yishma' El*). Two names, two fates, one God watching over both.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11v–12r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 21 begins in the right column of folio 11v (page 001), following immediately on the close of Genesis 20, and continues through folio 12r (page 002), where the chapter concludes at verse 34 and Genesis 22 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 16–17 (pa_0016, pa_0021; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0020 and /0021). The Vaticanus LXX text is visible in the manuscript images and diverges from the Leningrad Codex notably at verse 9, where the Greek adds "with her son Isaac" (μετὰ Ἰσαὰκ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς) as a specification of Ishmael's activity — a reading not attested in the Hebrew Masoretic tradition. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 21 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
